adult: Your brain files the day while you sleep. Skip it too often and things stop sticking u2014 meetings, names, where you left your keys.nnConsistent sleep beats one heroic ten-hour catch-up.
Light, meals, and bedtime all tell your body what time it is. Send mixed signals and your mood and focus will let you know about it.nnA dull wind-down routine is actually doing a lot of work.

Eating a big meal late at night tends to make blood-sugar control harder u2014 even if the total calories are identical to an earlier meal.nnItu2019s not about willpower. Itu2019s just how the body prefers its schedule.

Ten minutes of walking, cycling, or anything that gets you moving tends to clear the mental fog fairly reliably. Itu2019s one of the more reproducible findings in cognitive research.nnYou donu2019t need a full session. You just need to move.
Piggyback on what you already do

u201cAfter I make coffee, I do two minutes of stretching.u201d The existing habit does the reminding u2014 you donu2019t have to remember anything new.nnSmall links are more durable than big resolutions.
u201cIf this, then thatu201d really works

u201cIf itu2019s Tuesday at 7pm, I go to the gymu201d outperforms u201cI should exercise moreu201d every time. Specificity removes the daily decision.nnWriting the when and where is most of the work done.
Why checking a box feels good

Your brain pays attention to better-than-expected outcomes. A small visible win u2014 a tick, a streak u2014 registers as one. Thatu2019s not shallow; itu2019s just how learning works.nnAim that wiring at things worth repeating.

Some brains genuinely struggle to feel how long things take without external cues. Calendars, alarms, and timers arenu2019t crutches u2014 theyu2019re just the right tool for that particular wiring.nnVague plans fail specific people. Specificity is the fix.
Research is pretty consistent: self-criticism after a slip makes quitting more likely, not less. A calm restart keeps more people going long-term.nnFlat tyre. Fix it. Drive on.

Writing about tangled thoughts for a few minutes consistently reduces rumination in study after study. Youu2019re not trying to write well u2014 youu2019re just clearing the inbox.nnMessy notes count. Full sentences not required.
Regular readers carry larger vocabularies even when their formal education looks identical on paper. Fifteen quiet minutes a day compounds over years.nnSpeed-reading trophies optional.
Mindfulness practice produces modest but reliable gains on attention measures u2014 not magic, just practice applied to a specific skill.nnShort and daily beats long and occasional.

Spreading practice across days beats cramming it all into one session u2014 for skills, facts, and habits alike. Your brain consolidates better with gaps in between.nnShow up regularly. Let the gaps do half the work.
Who you think you are matters

People who say u201cIu2019m someone who exercisesu201d stick with it longer than people who say u201cIu2019m trying to exercise more.u201d Identity outruns motivation.nnThe story you tell on good days is the voice you hear on hard ones.
Shoes by the door. Gym bag already packed. Water bottle already filled. Environment design consistently beats willpower when youu2019re tired.nnReduce friction. Future-you will be grateful.
Other people help you show up
Knowing someone is loosely watching u2014 a friend, a partner, a group chat u2014 reliably nudges follow-through. You donu2019t need a coach.nnSometimes one text buddy is enough.

Under pressure, the brain defaults to its most familiar routines u2014 fast. If those routines are good ones, great. If not, stress will find that out quickly.nnSimplify the plan before stress does it for you.

The first thing poor sleep dulls is your ability to pause before reacting. Everything gets louder, smaller decisions feel bigger.nnProtecting sleep is protecting your judgement u2014 not a soft priority.
Short breaks from sitting improve energy ratings and help blood-sugar regulation after long desk stretches. The bar is genuinely low.nnStand up, walk to the kitchen, come back. That counts.
Real-world studies put habit formation at weeks to months, not the famous 21 days. Early repetitions are the investment. Ease is what you get later.nnYouu2019re not slow. Youu2019re just early.

People who treat ability as buildable come back after setbacks more readily than those who see it as fixed. A bad week is noise, not a ceiling.nnThe only thing a slip proves is that youu2019re still in the game.
Shifting the bulk of eating earlier in the day consistently shows better metabolic patterns in trials. Same food, different clock.nnTiming is a lever. Not a moral position.
Strength training supports bones, metabolism, and your ability to carry your own shopping decades from now. Two sessions a week is a legitimate longevity habit.nnNot about aesthetics. About function.

Every task switch leaves residual mental noise that slows down whatever comes next. Protecting a block of uninterrupted time isnu2019t a luxury u2014 itu2019s how actual deep work gets done.nnBatch the similar stuff. Guard the focused time.
A gap is not a verdict
Missing once doesnu2019t erase progress. Shame spirals do. The quickest recovery is the next small win u2014 not a Monday restart ceremony.nnComebacks count as data too.

Evening light from phones delays melatonin u2014 the signal that tells your body itu2019s time to wind down. Your brain canu2019t quite tell a screen from a sunny window.nnDimming helps. Distance helps more.

Mild dehydration u2014 the kind where you donu2019t feel particularly thirsty u2014 is enough to measurably dent concentration and mood. Most people walk around in it.nnFull water bottle on the desk. Boring and effective.
People consistently generate more ideas while walking than sitting still. If youu2019re stuck, a lap around the block is not procrastination.nnMove first. Judge the ideas when youu2019re back.
Large studies show people who cycle to work have meaningfully lower heart disease rates than those who drive the same route.nnNo lycra required.
Writing a goal down with a concrete plan consistently outperforms keeping it in your head. The act of writing it forces enough specificity to make it real.nnPaper is cheap accountability.

Switch tasks before finishing a thought and part of your brain stays on the old one. That leftover hum makes the new task harder than it actually is.nnJot a quick note before you jump. Costs ten seconds.
Start so small it feels silly
u201cTwo push-upsu201d beats u201cget fitu201d because itu2019s impossible to argue with. The first step should have no resistance at all.nnWin first. Scale later.
Rest is part of the program
Adaptation happens during recovery, not during effort. Skipping rest doesnu2019t add progress u2014 it just delays it and increases the chance something breaks.nnSchedule it. Treat it like a set.

Children in households with predictable routines show calmer and healthier patterns across multiple measures of wellbeing. Not rigid u2014 just consistent.nnYouu2019re building a felt sense of safety. Thatu2019s the whole point.
Just logging purchases u2014 without any budget lecture attached u2014 tends to reduce discretionary spending on its own. Awareness does most of the work.nnThe log is the intervention.

Self-control is not a fixed gift — it trains like anything else. Each time you follow through, the next start gets a little easier to reach.nnYou are not faking discipline; you are collecting receipts.

Sports science shows people often feel maxed out while their body still has a large reserve. The brain yells “stop” before the tank is empty.nnOn a brutal habit day, slow down — but question the story that you have nothing left.
Some nervous systems need louder outside cues — calendars, lists, apps — to feel time and reward fairly. That is biology, not laziness.nnUse the scaffolding that lets you live the life you chose.

The same circuits that light up for a gold star can light up for a workout or a slot machine. Healthy systems borrow that wiring on purpose.nnDirection matters more than the fact that chemistry notices.
Focus is a bundle of skills
Planning, starting, sticking, and switching are related but separate mental skills. They differ between people and can improve with practice.nnEvery steady week is quiet training for all of them.

When rewards arrive on an unpredictable rhythm, behaviour tends to stick harder than with a clockwork prize — that is old psychology lab wisdom.nnRandom insights here echo mild curiosity — separate from your weekly treats and pot rules, not an extra game layered on top.
Knowing someone else knows your goal nudges follow-through even if they never comment.nnPick an audience you trust; visibility is the tool.

Some people honestly mis-feel how long tasks take without clocks and reminders. That is not a character flaw — it is a mismatch between brain and vague plans.nnConcrete times and places are the kind fix.
“If X happens, I will do Y” plans routinely beat “I will try harder” in study after study.nnThe sentence is the intervention.
Rooms, layouts, and visible cues predict doing the habit better than how motivated you felt that morning.nnDesign for Tuesday-you, not Sunday-night-you.
People often follow what they think peers do, sometimes more than their private values say.nnUse that wisely: find a group where the normal thing is showing up.
A noticeable slice of adults meet criteria for attention-deficit conditions; many never get a label but still benefit from structure.nnIf a system helps you function, that is the outcome that counts.
Having several small dependable habits correlates with life satisfaction even when the habits themselves are humble.nnRhythm is its own kind of rest.
People restart goals more often after Mondays, month beginnings, or birthdays — “temporal landmarks” in the research.nnA Wednesday miss is not a season finale; Thursday can be episode two.

Industry data suggests only a sliver of people who download habit apps are still active two months later. Logging here already puts you on the uncommon side of the curve.nnCredit the structure you built, not luck.

Plenty of animals wake on cue, but only humans set an alarm, understand what it means, and then debate whether nine more minutes is “strategic rest.”nnThe snooze button is a tiny comedy about self-control every morning.
Over a life, sleep adds up to decades — more than many mortgages last. Unlike a mortgage, good sleep pays you back in mood and focus.nnTreat the mattress like infrastructure, not luxury.
The liver keeps its own clock
Roughly one standard drink per hour is what typical livers clear — coffee, cold showers, and pep talks do not speed the chemistry.nnPatience is the only life hack here.
Eleven days awake (please do not try)
A famous 1964 stunt stayed awake for eleven days under medical watch. He recovered — and said never again.nnYour late reel session is not that experiment, but it rhymes.
Gyms spike in early January, then quiet down by February. March regulars rarely have more “motivation” — they have a plan that survived February.nnSystems beat vibes.
The body moves before the story
Classic lab work found brain activity shifting before people reported a conscious choice to move. Much of life is noticing yourself mid-habit.nnAutomatic is not the enemy — it is the finish line.
People who make the bed often report feeling more on top of the day. Causation is fuzzy; the easy win is not.nnTwo minutes for a visible “already done” badge.
Some surveys find phones checked nearly a hundred times a day — tiny hits of novelty all waking hours.nnIf your habit app feels sticky, you are competing with world-class designers. Fair fight.
A mile of jogging burns less energy than a fancy pastry contains — annoying math, true math.nnStill worth it for heart, mood, and bones; health is not a spreadsheet duel.

“Deadline” once meant a line prisoners could not cross — serious consequences. Now it means spreadsheets.nnYour Week still has a firm line here: logging for that Mon–Sun row locks at midnight Sunday (local time) — gentler stakes, same human love of a boundary.
A well-known study found judges’ leniency dipped before meal breaks and rose after — probably fatigue and timing, not cruelty.nnYour 4pm self is not morally worse; just depleted.
The “three-week habit” story came from a surgeon’s guess decades ago. Real studies show weeks to many months depending on the habit and the person.nnYou are not behind — you are on a real curve.
Average humans walk enough miles in a life to circle Earth several times — mostly in small trips.nnThe “extra” walks for health are a rounding error that still changes risk.
Big studies link cycling to work with much lower heart-disease risk than driving the same route.nnLycra optional; pedals sufficient.
Muscles do not care if the weight has opinions. Carrying a toddler upstairs still counts as load.nnParenting: accidental gym.
Roman “average lifespan ~35” mostly reflects child deaths. Adults who survived childhood often reached their sixties and seventies.nnAverages hide different stories.
Most cells replace on a multi-year rhythm — you are a slow ship of Theseus.nnToday’s habits feed tomorrow’s replacement crew.
Putting things off often dodges boredom, anxiety, or shame — not a calendar glitch.nnName the feeling, shrink the task, start ugly.
Long-term stress nudges appetite toward quick calories — an old survival script.nnNot weakness; wiring you can plan around.
That urge to squeeze a puppy? Researchers gave it a name: cute aggression. Harmless, common, weirdly sweet.nnTotally unrelated to your streak — just a smile break.

Classic decision research found people hate losing about twice as much as they enjoy gaining the same amount. A clear “floor” uses that honesty: protecting progress hits different emotional notes than a vague “do better.”nnThe app names the stakes so your attention has somewhere real to stand.nnBelow the floor, recovery keeps rules transparent — core-only pot accrual and strict-drop resets — all explicit, with no random layer on top of your treats or pot.

In real-world trials, savers who chose accounts that locked funds until a date often built bigger balances than people left totally free — even when freedom sounds nicer.nnA named pot is a soft version of that contract with yourself.

Coffee-shop studies showed people speed up purchases as a free-drink card nears full — effort rises near the finish. A filling progress bar borrows the same nudge.nnClose targets feel magnetic; use that instead of guilt.

Economists model a pattern many people feel: distant rewards shrink in our minds while tonight’s treat grows huge. Pairing a small weekly win with a slow pot feeds both versions of you.nnStructure ends the argument instead of moralising it.

A giant review of health programmes found keeping track of behaviour — food, steps, sleep — was one of the strongest ingredients that made interventions work.nnYour taps are not theatre; they are a proven behaviour-change tool.
Michie et al. — behaviour change techniques
Earn the fun, enjoy it harder

Researchers boosted gym visits by pairing addictive audiobooks with treadmill time only. Your weekly treat after qualifying is the same bargain: joy rides on the habit, not beside it.nnPleasure with a gate stays memorable longer.

Give someone a random mug and they suddenly price it like treasure. Streaks and pots inherit that quirk — losing ground stings because it feels like giving something up.nnThe design respects that feeling instead of pretending you are a spreadsheet.

Self-regulation theories say we compare “where I am” to “where I want to be,” then adjust. Colours, chips, and week rows are that comparison made friendly.nnYou get direction without drowning in shame.
Labels change spending in your head

People treat “holiday money” and “bill money” as if they are not interchangeable — even when the bank account is one. Separate goal pots ride that habit so wins stay attached to their story.nnCompartmentalising can be a feature.

Brain studies show cells that learn from “better than expected” moments. A pop-up fact you did not schedule is a mild version of that — curiosity without turning insights into a wager.nnSmall novelty can support focus when kept kind.
The green pill around your name shows that you are currently online. When it's grey it means that you have lost connection, but your data will keep being saved on your device until next sync.

When consistency jumps straight from struggling to solid, it usually means several closed weeks lined up behind the number — not one fluke week.nnLet the colour change be a quiet “keep going,” not pressure to be perfect next week.

Early in a month, people often lean on fresh-start energy. Research on “temporal landmarks” shows Mondays and month beginnings nudge new attempts without needing a dramatic reboot.nnIf your goals sit in the steady band, you are allowed to ride that gentle tailwind.

Big studies on habits stress boring repetition over hero weeks. A new calendar year is just another Monday in a longer chain — useful if it helps you show up, not if it becomes all-or-nothing.nnSteady beats dramatic when the year gets noisy.

Maintenance research keeps repeating one lesson: slips are normal; re-entry predicts long-term change more than a spotless streak.nnOpening the log again after a rough week is already the behaviour scientists care about.
Habits added mid-week get a fair start

If you add a habit on a day other than Monday, that first partial week uses proportional targets — your threshold is scaled down based on how many days remain in the week.nnFor example, if your goal is 5 days per week and you start on Thursday, you only need to hit roughly 2 days (floor of 5 × 4/7). This keeps scoring fair without blocking your goal on a week you could never fully complete.

Core habits must all qualify for a closed week to count toward your % and weekly treats; growth habits add pot money when they qualify but never block the week. In recovery (rolling % below that goal’s consistency floor), only core habits add £ to the pot; growth adds £0 until rolling % reaches the floor again. Each goal’s floor is set in Add/Edit goal (default often 80%).

Popular books like “The Molecule of More” and “Dopamine Nation” stress one idea: much of what people call “reward” is wanting and momentum, not the cozy feeling after a good meal.nnChasing the next tick is dopamine-heavy; deep rest after is often other chemistry.
Two styles of dopamine, one you

Lieberman and Long describe a “here-and-now” chemical chorus and a “future-and-possible” dopamine track that loves novelty and goals — both matter.nnBalance is the game: enough future-pull to plan, enough present chemistry to enjoy arriving.
Lieberman & Long — The Molecule of More

Dopamine-related circuits learn from surprises and progress signals — “better than expected” keeps attention. That is why visible streaks and near-complete bars tug harder than abstract goals.nnYou are not weak; you are using a loud channel wisely.
Schultz et al.; Lieberman & Long (popular synthesis)
Pleasure and pain share a balance beam

Psychiatrist Anna Lembke argues endless easy hits — scrolls, snacks, buys — can tilt the brain toward needing more just to feel normal. Brief, chosen discomfort (effort, delay) resets the scale.nnHabit apps that add friction on purpose echo that clinical lesson gently.
Oxytocin is a trust hormone, not only a cuddle hormone

Studies tie oxytocin to bonding cues: touch, warm voice, shared meals. It nudges social calm and “we” feelings.nnAccountability partners and family habits may ride social chemistry, not only willpower.
Oxytocin has more than one mood

Researchers note context matters: the same hormone that supports closeness can sharpen feelings about “us versus them” in some settings.nnKindness with boundaries stays wise — chemistry is not a morality tale.
Serotonin is a stabiliser, not a smile pill

Most brain serotonin does not come with simple labels. It helps regulate mood, sleep timing, and even gut signalling — a steadying hand, not a happy switch.nnMedication stories are separate from daily habits; both deserve nuance.

Exercise and outdoor light are linked to better mood and sleep in large studies — serotonin-related pathways are part of the story, alongside many other systems.nnA walk is low-tech support for a complicated brain.

After intense pleasure or orgasm, prolactin often rises — part of why excitement eases and the body pivots toward rest. It is normal biology, not failure.nnUnderstanding the cooldown helps you plan recovery, not judge it.
Prolactin shows up for caregivers too
Hormone research links prolactin to nurturing behaviours in many mammals — milk production in some contexts and caregiving tone more broadly.nnBodies reward tending others with quiet chemistry.

Sapolsky’s “Behave” walks one behaviour through genes, hormones, context, and culture — dopamine, serotonin, oxytocin, and more act in concert like instruments, not soloists.nnWhen you change a habit, you are conducting the whole band slowly.
Books beat doom-scrolling for the same circuits

Writers like Lembke and Lieberman describe how cheap digital hits train dopamine shortcuts. Swapping some scroll time for long-form explanations rebuilds patience — the same patience habits need.nnThis app’s slower rewards are a cousin of that swap.

Research on habit formation shows “automaticity” emerges over weeks to months, with wide individual differences — not a single fixed number for everyone.nnEarning this tier means your logged behaviour met the bar over roughly a month of calendar days for this habit — celebrate showing up.

As windows lengthen, off-days age out of the lookback while you keep accumulating proof you can return.nnThis tier is about sustaining the pattern on the Log across roughly two months of calendar days.
Mastery III — you own this here

Meeting a high bar over the longest mastery window (calendar days) in this app is rare air — you have built real consistency with this habit here.nnConsider banking it for now and revisiting in 6–12 months if you want to reinforce it again.
A long pause after mastery is normal life. Choosing to return and meet the maintenance bar shows the habit is still yours — a deliberate reunion, not a rescue mission.
Years later, the signal is not perfection. It is that you came back and let the weeks add up again — quiet proof that the identity stuck.
Half a decade is enough seasons to fail, wander, and restart. Earning this means the pattern survived real life — not a streak fantasy.
A decade marker is as much about values as behaviour: some practices deserve to stay loosely tethered across everything else that changes.

For a small child, time has two settings: this moment, and everything else u2014 which feels like infinity. Thatu2019s not immaturity. Itu2019s how the brain is wired before it learns to feel the distance between today and next week.nnHabits are how you train that distance to feel real.
Jean Piaget u2014 developmental psychology; Gabor Matu00e9, Scattered Minds

The young brain defaults to magical thinking u2014 want something hard enough and it arrives. Most adults still carry a version of this around goals.nnThe system youu2019re building here is the direct answer to that: small repeated actions, not intention.
Jean Piaget; Gabor Matu00e9, Scattered Minds

No one is born understanding that todayu2019s choices shape next week. That link u2014 cause, then effect, across time u2014 is something the brain constructs gradually. In some people that construction takes longer.nnThatu2019s not a deficit. Itu2019s just the starting point.
Jean Piaget u2014 cognitive development; Gabor Matu00e9, Scattered Minds

Piaget found that children donu2019t have a full grasp of time until around age seven. Before that, duration and consequence are genuinely hard to feel u2014 not a behaviour choice.nnFor some brains, external structure does what that internal clock hasnu2019t fully built yet.
Jean Piaget u2014 preoperational stage; Gabor Matu00e9, Scattered Minds
You canu2019t bend time by wanting to

Piaget observed that young children believe they can stop, speed up, or slow time down. That instinct doesnu2019t always fully leave. If deadlines feel like suggestions and schedules seem to shift around you u2014 thatu2019s the same wiring, older.nnStructure isnu2019t a workaround. Itu2019s the actual solution.
Jean Piaget u2014 preoperational stage; Gabor Matu00e9, Scattered Minds

kid: When you sleep, your brain sorts the day — a bit like tidying your room.\n\nIf you miss sleep a lot, remembering gets harder. A usual bedtime beats one huge catch-up night.
Sunlight, meals, and bedtime tell your body what time it is. Mixed signals can make you feel off.\n\nA calm wind-down routine is doing real work, even if it feels boring.
A very big meal late at night can make blood sugar trickier to handle — even with the same food earlier.\n\nIt is biology, not “being bad.”
About ten minutes of walking or moving often clears a fuzzy head.\n\nYou do not need a huge workout — wiggle, walk, dance.
Hook new habits to old ones
Try: “After I brush my teeth, I do two stretches.” The old habit reminds you.\n\nTiny links beat giant promises.
“Tuesday after school I ride my bike” beats “I should move more.”\n\nBrains love a clear plan.
Your brain likes little wins — checkmarks, stars, streaks.\n\nUse that power for habits that help you.
Some brains need clocks, timers, or lists to feel how long things take. That is okay — tools are helpers.\n\n“Someday” is fuzzy; “after snack” is friendly.
Being mean to yourself after a slip makes it harder to try again.\n\nFix it like a flat bike tyre: patch it and ride on.
A few minutes of messy writing can untangle worried thoughts.\n\nYou do not need perfect sentences.
Reading a little most days grows vocabulary over time.\n\nShort, cosy reading still counts.
Calm attention practice can train focus a bit like practice for sport.\n\nShort and often beats rare and long.
A bit on many days beats one giant cram for skills and habits.\n\nGaps between tries help your brain learn.
Saying “I am a reader” or “I move my body” can stick better than “I am trying.”\n\nTell a story that helps you on hard days.
Lay out clothes, fill a water bottle, put shoes by the door — easy setups beat arguing with a tired brain.\n\nLess friction, more follow-through.
When someone kind knows your plan, you are more likely to do it.\n\nA friend, parent, or coach counts.
When stressed, brains reach for familiar routines fast — good or not.\n\nKeep your plan simple before stress arrives.
Poor sleep makes it harder to pause before you snap or give up.\n\nSleep protects your choices.
Short breaks from sitting help energy and sugar control.\n\nStand, stretch, grab water — it counts.
Real life habits often need weeks or months, not magic “21 days.”\n\nEarly tries are not failure — they are the start.
People who believe skills can grow bounce back after a bad day.\n\nA rough week is a bump, not the end.
More food earlier in the day often suits the body better than only huge late meals.\n\nTiming is a tool, not a rule about “good” or “bad.”
Strength helps bones and everyday jobs like carrying bags.\n\nTwo short sessions a week is a big win.
Switching tasks leaves mental “echoes” that slow you down.\n\nGive one job a protected block of time.
A miss is not the end
Skipping once does not erase progress. Shame spirals do more damage.\n\nThe next small win is the comeback.
Bright screens at night can delay sleepy chemistry.\n\nDim screens or move them farther away before bed.
Even mild thirst can ding mood and attention.\n\nKeep water handy — boring and useful.
Walking often brings more ideas than sitting.\n\nStuck? Try a lap before deciding.
Big studies link biking with healthier hearts than only driving.\n\nPedals do not need fancy clothes.
Goals with a written plan beat goals only in your head.\n\nPaper or phone notes both work.
Finish a thought before you jump
If you switch tasks mid-thought, part of your brain stays behind.\n\nJot a note in ten seconds, then switch.
“Two jumps” beats “get fit forever” for actually starting.\n\nMake step one too easy to argue with.
Muscles and skills grow during recovery, not only during effort.\n\nRest is part of training.
Predictable rhythms help kids feel safe and healthy — not rigid, just steady.\n\nYou are building a calm home feeling.
Just noticing what you buy often lowers extra spending — no lecture needed.\n\nThe list is the magic.
Following through trains “stop and go” skills like sport practice.\n\nEach small keep-trying counts.
Bodies often have more gas than the brain admits on hard days.\n\nSlow down, but question “I have nothing left.”
Some brains need louder outside reminders. That is wiring, not laziness.\n\nUse what works for you.
Stars, ticks, and games use similar “win” wiring — healthy apps borrow that on purpose.\n\nSteer the wins toward good habits.
Planning, starting, sticking, and switching are different skills.\n\nSteady weeks quietly train all of them.
Random little rewards can make habits stickier than perfect clocks.\n\nThis app’s surprise facts are gentle curiosity — separate from weekly treats and jar rules, not an extra game on top.
When a trusted person knows your goal, you are more likely to try.\n\nThey do not have to nag.

“If it rains, I dance inside” beats “I will try harder.”\n\nThe sentence is the superpower.
Visible cues and layouts predict habits better than morning excitement.\n\nDesign for Tuesday-you.
People often copy what friends seem to do.\n\nPick friends or groups where showing up is normal.
Many people have attention differences; structure still helps everyone.\n\nIf a tool helps you live your plan, it wins.
Several small steady habits link to happier days — even humble ones.\n\nRhythm can feel like rest.
Mondays, month starts, and birthdays nudge new tries.\n\nA Wednesday miss is not the finale.

Only humans set alarms… then negotiate nine more minutes.\n\nThe snooze button is a tiny comedy about mornings.

A clear “floor” in the app makes the stakes honest.

Below the floor, recovery keeps jar rules clear — core-only money until the score recovers. Ask a grown-up for the full picture.
Savers who chose “locked until a date” accounts often saved more than total freedom.\n\nGoal pots here echo that gentle lock.
People speed up when a punch-card is almost full.\n\nProgress bars use the same “almost there” pull.
Tonight’s treat feels huge; faraway rewards feel tiny.\n\nWeekly treats plus a slow pot feed both versions of you.
Big reviews say logging food, steps, or sleep is one of the strongest helpers in health programmes.\n\nYour taps are real tools.
Researchers paired audiobooks with treadmill time only — visits went up.\n\nYour weekly treat after qualifying uses the same bargain.
We care extra about things we feel we own — streaks and pots use that quirk.\n\nThe app respects feelings, not only numbers.
Brains compare “where I am” to “where I want to be,” then adjust.\n\nColours and chips make that comparison kind.
People treat “holiday money” and “bill money” differently even in one account.\n\nSeparate goal pots ride that habit.
Brains learn from “better than expected” moments.\n\nSurprise facts here are mild curiosity — little brain teasers, not bets.
Green around your name means you are online! Grey means you are not!
Jumping from struggling to solid usually means several good closed weeks — not one fluke week.\n\nLet the colour change say “keep going,” not “be perfect.”
Month starts give a fresh-start boost for many people.\n\nIf you are in the steady band, ride the gentle tailwind.
Habits love boring repetition more than hero weeks.\n\nA new year is useful if it helps you show up — not if it becomes all-or-nothing.
Slips are normal. Scientists care most that you return.\n\nOpening the log after a rough week already counts.
If you add a habit mid-week, your first partial week uses a smaller target so it stays fair.\n\nExample: starting Thursday with a 5-day goal might ask ~2 days that week.

Core habits are your must-do habits: when a week closes, they all need to qualify so the week counts on your score and for your treat.

Growth habits add jar money when you do them — they never block the week.

In recovery (below your floor), only core adds jar money until your score climbs back; growth waits. A grown-up can help with the numbers.
A lot of what people call “reward” is wanting and go-go energy, not only cosy afterglow.\n\nChasing the next tick uses different chemistry than deep rest.
Writers describe “here-and-now” chemistry and “future-and-maybe” chemistry — both matter.\n\nBalance planning with enjoying the moment.
Brains learn from surprises and progress signals.\n\nVisible streaks use a loud, honest channel — not a weakness.
Endless quick hits — scrolls, snacks — can tilt the brain toward needing more to feel normal.\n\nChosen effort and breaks can reset the scale.
Oxytocin links to bonding cues: touch, warm voices, shared meals.\n\nFamily habits ride social chemistry, not only willpower.
The same hormone can nudge closeness or “us vs them” feelings depending on context.\n\nKindness plus boundaries stays wise.
Serotonin helps mood, sleep timing, and even gut signals — a stabiliser, not a simple “happy switch.”\n\nMedicine stories are separate from daily habits.
Exercise and outdoor light link to better mood and sleep in big studies.\n\nA walk is simple support for a complicated brain.
After very intense fun, the body shifts toward calm chemistry — that is normal biology.\n\nUnderstanding cooldown helps you plan rest, not worry.
Hormone research ties prolactin to nurturing behaviours in many mammals.\n\nBodies quietly reward caring for others.
One behaviour involves genes, hormones, context, and culture together — like a band, not one instrument.\n\nChanging a habit slowly conducts the whole band.
Cheap digital hits train shortcuts; longer reading rebuilds patience.\n\nThis app’s slower rewards echo that gentler pace.

adult: The app that helps you discover, build, and become your better self.
Get ready to change your life one week at a time. Here you’ll grow the consistency you always needed but couldn’t quite stick with.
You can edit your name and add your birth date. If your age is 0–8 you’ll have a different experience. Tracking will start from today.

kid: The app that helps you discover, build, and become your better self.
Get ready to grow good habits one week at a time — small steps that add up.
You can edit your name and add your birthday. If you are 0–8 things look a bit friendlier. Tracking starts from today — a grown-up can help with dates.

adult: A few things worth knowing before you start.

This app helps you build consistency for everything you struggle with until now, using three mechanisms:

  • Goal — long-term rewards so you keep your eyes on what you are saving for.
  • Weekly treat — short-term rewards so you feel the win often.
  • Consistency — a streak you will want to keep going.
Consistency is built on mundane repetition. But this app makes it interesting and fun. Life is neither easy nor perfect, but we think it’s OK to be on top of things at least 80% of the time.
Qualify your week to remain Consistent for 80% of your tracking time and you can reap your big Goal reward, on top of building the good habits you really need.

A week qualifies by qualifying its (core) habits. More on this later.

You are now ready to set your first Goal.

kid: A few things worth knowing before you start.

This app uses three ideas:

  • A big Goal to save for.
  • A small weekly Treat when you do well.
  • A Consistency streak you want to protect.

Ask a grown-up if a sentence feels tricky.
Consistency means showing up again and again. This app tries to make that feel fair and a bit fun. Aim to be on track most weeks — grown-ups often use about 80% as a guide.
When your week qualifies, you stay Consistent over your tracked weeks and move toward your big Goal while building habits.

A week qualifies when its core habits qualify. Grown-ups can read more in Settings → Help.

Next: add your first Goal.

adult: Name something you’re working toward — often something you could save toward over weeks or months. Think inspiring target, not a vague chore.

kid: Pick something big you want — like saving for something special. Make it something that makes you feel excited.

adult: This is a small reward for a good week — like a film night or a favourite snack. It’s separate from your big goal; it isn’t for weight-style outcomes.

kid: Pick a little treat for a good week — like a fun outing or a small thing you like. Keep it small and fun, not big scary goals.

adult: Use small repeatable actions — like “walk 20 minutes” or “read before bed”. Avoid vague outcomes (“lose 30kg”), and don’t use your weekly treat as a habit.

kid: Write a small thing you do again and again — like “read 10 minutes”. Big goals and treats go in the other boxes.

adult:
Core — Every core habit must qualify in a week for that week to count as "on track".

Growth — Both core and growth habits add money to your pots at the end of the week when they qualify.

Recovery mode — When your consistency % drops below the floor, only core habits add to your pot until you recover (more on this later).

kid: Pick your Tier: core or growth

Core habits are the big “must-do” ones for a goal. After a week closes, every core habit for that goal needs to qualify so that week counts on your score circle and for your weekly treat.

Growth habits are extra helpful habits. When they qualify, they add jar money — but missing them never blocks the week from counting.

Recovery means your rolling score dropped below your floor (grown-ups pick the number per goal; it is often around 80%). Then only core habits add jar money until your score climbs back to the floor; growth habits wait and add £0 so you focus on the must-dos first.

Ask a grown-up to open Tips in Settings if you want the longer friendly guide.
